text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE
Head and Neck Multidisciplinary Oncology Program

---

REFERRING SERVICE: Otolaryngology / Head and Neck Surgery
REASON FOR CONSULTATION: New diagnosis of HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue; tumor board review and treatment planning.

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Chen is a 52-year-old East Asian woman, never-smoker, presenting for medical oncology consultation following a new diagnosis of HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue with ipsilateral level IIA nodal involvement. She was referred following multidisciplinary head and neck tumor board review for treatment planning discussion.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen initially presented to her primary care physician several months ago with approximately 3 months of persistent left-sided throat discomfort and a sensation of something stuck in the back of her throat. She had mild pain with swallowing but was able to eat solid foods without significant difficulty. There was no hoarseness and no ear pain at that time. She was treated empirically with amoxicillin-clavulanate for presumed pharyngitis; symptoms did not improve. She returned approximately 2 months later with continued throat discomfort, now accompanied by left-sided otalgia. On that visit, her PCP noted a firm, non-tender approximately 2 cm left neck mass and referred her urgently to Otolaryngology.

Subsequent workup included a CT neck with contrast demonstrating a 2.1 x 1.8 cm left level IIA cervical lymph node with internal heterogeneity and loss of fatty hilum, and asymmetric fullness of the left base of tongue. MRI neck confirmed a T2-hyperintense, gadolinium-enhancing left base of tongue mass measuring 2.2 x 1.9 x 1.7 cm, with the left level IIA node measuring 2.1 x 1.8 cm with central necrosis; no radiographic extranodal extension was identified. PET-CT (18F-FDG) demonstrated an FDG-avid left base of tongue primary (SUVmax 12.4) and a single hypermetabolic left level IIA lymph node (SUVmax 9.8); no contralateral nodal or distant metastatic disease was identified.

Direct laryngoscopy with biopsy confirmed squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, arising from the left base of tongue with diffuse strong p16 overexpression (>70% tumor nuclei and cytoplasm, block positive) and HPV type 16 confirmed on in situ hybridization. FNA of the left level IIA node showed metastatic squamous cell carcinoma, also p16 positive. Molecular profiling by 500-gene targeted NGS panel showed PIK3CA p.E545K activating mutation (VAF 28%), TRAF3 truncating mutation, KMT2D loss-of-function mutation, EP300 frameshift, FBXW7 p.R465C, FAT1 truncating mutation, and NOTCH1 truncating mutation; TP53 was wild-type, consistent with HPV-related disease. PD-L1 CPS was 18. TMB 7.2 mut/Mb. No EGFR, KRAS, or FGFR alterations. MSI-stable.

The case was presented at our head and neck multidisciplinary tumor board today. Surgical, radiation oncology, medical oncology, pathology, radiology, and speech-language pathology were all represented. After review of all imaging, pathology, and molecular data, the consensus of the tumor board was to recommend definitive concurrent chemoradiation with curative intent. Surgical resection was discussed but not recommended: for T2N1 HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC in a never-smoker, function-preserving concurrent chemoradiation achieves excellent disease control rates comparable to surgery, with lower rates of long-term dysphagia and without the morbidity of transoral robotic surgery or neck dissection in this clinical context.

Patient was seen today in consultation to discuss the treatment plan in full.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. No prior malignancies
2. No known autoimmune disease
3. No prior head and neck surgery or radiation
4. Hypothyroidism — not yet diagnosed (see below; at-risk given planned neck radiation)
5. Denies hypertension, diabetes, coronary artery disease, chronic kidney disease

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- Direct laryngoscopy with biopsy (recent, per Otolaryngology)
- No other significant surgical history

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Ms. Chen works as a high school music teacher. She is married and lives at home with her spouse and two adult children who are both supportive. She is a lifelong never-smoker. Alcohol use is minimal (fewer than 1 drink per week). She denies illicit drug use. She is of East Asian descent. No occupational exposures of concern.

FAMILY HISTORY:
No family history of head and neck cancer. No known hereditary cancer syndromes. Father had hypertension; mother alive and healthy.

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS:
1. No regular prescription medications prior to this diagnosis
2. Occasional ibuprofen PRN for headache
3. Multivitamin daily

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive for: persistent left-sided throat discomfort and odynophagia (mild, able to swallow soft foods and liquids), left-sided otalgia (referred), palpable left neck mass (noted by PCP and ENT). Mild fatigue.
Negative for: dysphagia to solid foods (currently able to eat), hoarseness, voice change, hemoptysis, epistaxis, significant weight loss (reports approximately 2 lbs lost over prior 4 months), shortness of breath, chest pain, neurologic deficits, trismus, diplopia, facial numbness. No B-symptoms (no drenching night sweats, no fevers). No recent dental infections.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 36.7°C. Weight: 62 kg. Height: 163 cm. BMI: 23.3 kg/m2. ECOG performance status: 1.

General: Well-appearing East Asian woman in no acute distress. Alert and fully oriented. Appropriately anxious given new diagnosis but engaged in discussion.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No facial asymmetry. Oral mucosa moist; dentition in reasonable condition per recent dental evaluation. No trismus. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy performed by ENT documents exophytic left base of tongue lesion ~2 cm, friable; not repeated today. No visible mucosal lesion in the anterior oral cavity or hard palate.

Neck: Firm, mobile, non-tender approximately 2 cm lymph node in the left level IIA region. No matting, no overlying skin changes, no fixation to underlying structures on palpation. No right-sided lymphadenopathy. No supraclavicular lymphadenopathy bilaterally. Thyroid non-palpable.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No stridor.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal deficits. Normal strength and sensation in all four extremities.

Skin: No rashes. No jaundice.

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS:
CBC: WBC 6.8 x10^9/L (differential unremarkable), Hgb 13.4 g/dL, Plt 228 x10^9/L
CMP: Na 139, K 4.0, Cl 103, CO2 24, BUN 14, Creatinine 0.78 mg/dL, eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2, Glucose 88, Total protein 7.1, Albumin 4.1 g/dL, ALT 22 U/L, AST 19 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, Alk phos 74 U/L
Magnesium: 1.9 mEq/L (normal)
TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (normal baseline; will monitor after radiation given field including lower neck)
Audiogram: Normal hearing bilaterally across all tested frequencies. No pre-existing sensorineural hearing loss.
LFTs: Within normal limits as above.
HBsAg, anti-HCV: Negative (screened prior to potential cisplatin-based immunosuppression in this ethnic background).

KEY IMAGING SUMMARY:
- CT neck with contrast: Left level IIA node 2.1 x 1.8 cm, left BOT fullness
- PET-CT: FDG-avid left BOT (SUVmax 12.4), left level IIA node (SUVmax 9.8); M0
- MRI neck: Left BOT mass 2.2 x 1.9 x 1.7 cm, no pre-epiglottic or vallecula extension, no radiographic ENE
- Collectively consistent with T2N1M0 disease, AJCC 8th ed HPV-positive staging: Stage III

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Margaret Chen is a 52-year-old never-smoking woman with a new diagnosis of Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive classification) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, p16-positive, HPV-16 confirmed by ISH. Overall, she has an excellent functional baseline (ECOG 1) and favorable tumor biology.

TREATMENT RECOMMENDATION (per multidisciplinary tumor board consensus):
Definitive concurrent chemoradiation with curative intent.

1. RADIATION THERAPY:
   - Intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT), 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the primary tumor and gross nodal disease (high-risk PTV)
   - Elective nodal coverage to bilateral neck per standard field design for oropharyngeal SCC
   - Image guidance with daily cone-beam CT
   - Radiation Oncology will see the patient separately for simulation planning and consent
   - Parotid-sparing technique to be utilized to minimize long-term xerostomia

2. SYSTEMIC THERAPY (concurrent radiosensitizer):
   - Cisplatin 100 mg/m2 IV every 3 weeks, administered on weeks 1, 4, and 7 of radiation (up to 3 cycles)
   - Pre-medications: ondansetron, dexamethasone, aggressive IV hydration (1-2L NS pre/post cisplatin), and scheduled antiemetics for days 2-5 following each cycle
   - Rationale for cisplatin over cetuximab: she is a cisplatin-eligible patient (creatinine 0.78 mg/dL, GFR adequate, normal hearing, good PS, age 52); multiple randomized trials and meta-analyses demonstrate superior locoregional control with high-dose cisplatin over cetuximab in HPV-positive patients treated with definitive RT

3. DE-ESCALATION TRIAL ELIGIBILITY:
   - Discussed de-escalation approaches (e.g., ECOG 3311-type reduced-dose RT arms, PATHOS trial concepts, carboplatin substitution, omission of chemotherapy in select cases) given favorable biology (HPV+, never-smoker, Stage III)
   - After review of molecular profiling, enrollment in a de-escalation protocol is not being recommended at this time: the presence of an activating PIK3CA p.E545K mutation introduces uncertainty regarding prognosis within the favorable-risk HPV-positive category, and we prefer to proceed with standard-of-care treatment
   - The patient was counseled that the existing data strongly supports standard cisplatin-based chemoradiation for her stage and that outcomes are expected to be excellent

4. SUPPORTIVE CARE AND PROPHYLAXIS:
   - Dental evaluation: completed (pre-radiation dental clearance obtained)
   - Audiogram: obtained today — normal, acceptable for cisplatin
   - Speech-language pathology: to evaluate baseline swallowing function prior to treatment initiation; counseling on expected toxicities and swallowing exercises
   - Nutritional evaluation: registered dietitian referral placed; baseline BMI and albumin adequate; prophylactic PEG tube placement will be discussed but deferred per patient preference for now; will reassess if oral intake declines significantly during treatment
   - Anti-emetics: scheduled ondansetron 8 mg PO TID on days of and for 3 days following cisplatin; prochlorperazine PRN
   - Magnesium monitoring: cisplatin causes renal magnesium wasting; will check Mg with each cycle
   - Nephroprotection: IV hydration protocol with mannitol as per institutional cisplatin protocol; will monitor creatinine closely; hold cisplatin if creatinine rises >25% above baseline or eGFR falls below threshold
   - Myeloid growth factor: not routinely indicated for concurrent chemoradiation at this dose intensity

5. HYPOTHYROIDISM MONITORING:
   - TSH currently normal (2.1 mIU/L)
   - Bilateral lower neck within radiation field; radiation-induced hypothyroidism expected in 20-40% of patients; will check TSH every 3-6 months following treatment completion

6. GOALS OF TREATMENT AND PROGNOSIS:
   - Curative intent. Published data for T2N1 HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC treated with definitive chemoradiation demonstrate 3-year overall survival rates in the range of 90-95% and excellent locoregional control
   - Expected toxicities were reviewed in detail with the patient and her spouse: mucositis (expected grade 2-3 during treatment weeks 3-6), dysphagia (likely requiring soft diet; PEG tube may be needed), xerostomia (expected long-term but often gradually improves), radiation dermatitis, fatigue, nausea/vomiting (cisplatin-related), ototoxicity risk, nephrotoxicity risk, peripheral neuropathy risk
   - Long-term: fibrosis of the neck soft tissues, potential for late dysphagia, hypothyroidism, hearing changes
   - Patient verbalized understanding and expressed strong preference to proceed with recommended treatment plan
   - Informed consent for cisplatin obtained today

7. FOLLOW-UP:
   - Radiation Oncology consultation and CT simulation scheduling in progress
   - Speech-language pathology evaluation scheduled prior to treatment start
   - Dietitian consultation placed
   - Week 1 of chemoradiation targeted to begin within approximately 2-3 weeks pending simulation and final plan approval
   - Medical Oncology to follow throughout chemoradiation with weekly check-ins and labs on cisplatin weeks; nursing to coordinate pre-chemotherapy bloodwork and hydration scheduling

Total time with patient: approximately 75 minutes including examination, chart review, discussion of pathology and imaging, and treatment planning discussion. Patient's spouse was present throughout.

Attending Medical Oncologist
Head and Neck Multidisciplinary Oncology Program
